danger_signals.visual_form: Oume Gōketsuji appears as unplayable boss with distinct visual treatment (palette-swap of Otane but elder presentation)
danger_signals.meaning: Authority figure and primary threat to player progression in tournament

danger_signals.visual_form: Opponent selection screen changes as player wins matches
danger_signals.meaning: Escalating difficulty and danger as tournament progresses toward final confrontation

safety_signals.visual_form: Menu option for training without combat stakes
safety_signals.meaning: Safe space to learn mechanics before entering competitive tournament

safety_signals.visual_form: Eight selectable characters with varied fighting styles and appearances
safety_signals.meaning: Player agency in choosing preferred combat approach and character identity

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Gōketsuji family name/crest (implied through character naming and clan tournament structure)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Oume as elder authority figure vs. Otane as younger challenger; boss palette distinguishes authority
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Tournament setting serves as clan-specific context; Japanese cultural framing of family hierarchy

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: International characters: Angela (Italian), Annie (English), Keith (implied Western)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Non-family members visually distinct by nationality/cultural aesthetics in character design
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Characters compete in Gōketsuji tournament despite external origin, indicating cross-cultural martial arts competition

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Succession crisis - 78-year-old Oume requires replacement as clan head
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Tournament bracket structure; Oume positioned as final boss/authority to challenge
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: All eight characters motivated to compete; fighting style variations reflect individual personality and background

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Family power struggle - Otane vs. Oume sibling conflict
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Otane and Oume appear as palette-swaps visually, but Oume positioned as superior authority; distinct character endings differentiate outcomes
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Otane's narrative driven by desire to prove worth against abusive elder sister; character motivations diverge based on clan involvement

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Absurdist social dynamics - humor-driven narrative context
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character roster spans absurd archetypes: strongwoman, spoiled aristocrat, playboy, superhuman family members
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Characters retain comedic personality in win quotes and endings; tone signals low stakes despite tournament framing

environmental_storytelling.technique: Character biography pop-ups between demo matches
environmental_storytelling.example: Japanese version displays character backstories during attract mode to establish motivations (omitted in English version)
Oume speaks to player before confrontation, changing dialogue based on opponent (e.g., different lines vs. sister Otane)
Japanese version features character-specific endings reflecting personal story arcs; English version reduces to generic congratulations screen, diminishing world-state narrative
Japanese version includes specific victory dialogue for each defeated opponent (contextual); English version reduces to single generic quote per character

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Unplayable character positioned as tournament finale; superhuman fighting abilities; dialogue shifts based on opponent identity
Motivated by years of abuse to prove worthiness; combat style reflects trauma-driven determination; narrative arc leads toward confrontation with Oume
Strongwoman persona; flirtatious/passionate attitude; international outsider perspective; no direct clan stakes
Calm, compassionate but overbearing due to privilege; romantic subplot with Keith; kept exotic pets; non-combative personality despite fighting game participation
Playboy archetype initially; character development driven by romantic interest in Annie; motivation shifts from superficial to sincere commitment

notes: Power Instinct communicates world-state primarily through narrative framing (succession crisis) rather than visual environmental design typical of fighting games. The dual-nature of the Gōketsuji clan (family hierarchy) versus international tournament creates faction tension without explicit color-coding or sigil systems. Regional censorship/omission of Japanese version content (character biographies, contextual win quotes, pre-fight scenes, endings) significantly diminished English version's ability to communicate political/social narrative layers. Absurdist tone signals low narrative stakes despite serious succession premise. Transformation feature (mentioned in Wikipedia but not detailed in sources) likely provides additional visual manifestation of character state/power.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Power Instinct SNES)
wikipedia (Power Instinct series overview and character descriptions)
